metadata.title: How to Animate a Character Who Feels
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: How to Animate a Character Who Feels
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Making a character feel is a measurement question, not a creative one. Conveying emotion is a translation problem with fixed, learnable rules. A model sheet is a document of facts, not a piece of art. Following those fixed translation rules is what makes a character read as feeling.

artifactLines: Making a character feel is a measurement question, not a creative one.
Conveying emotion is a translation problem with fixed, learnable rules.
A model sheet is a document of facts, not a piece of art.
Following those fixed translation rules is what makes a character read as feeling.

beats.narration_text: The reframe that making a character feel is a measurement and translation problem, not a creative one, and that a model sheet is a document of facts with fixed translation rules, changes how I'd approach animation. Help me: (1) explain what exactly is being measured when we say emotion is measured, not invented; (2) give an example translation rule mapping an internal state to a concrete pose or timing; (3) clarify why the model sheet must be facts rather than art.

props.segment: How to Animate a Character Who Feels

output: This is not a creative question
Does the character have a petite frame or a lanky one
Pick five colors maximum for a character’s core look
